exemplar.title: How does changing the mass of the initial cart (100g, 200g, 300g, 400g, 500g, 600g) affect the coefficient of restitution of the collision with the second cart of 100g at rest.

detailedExemplarData.title: How does changing the mass of the initial cart (100g, 200g, 300g, 400g, 500g, 600g) affect the coefficient of restitution of the collision with the second cart of 100g at rest.

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are clearly presented with correct headings, units and significant figures.
Sample calculations for averages and uncertainty propagation are shown and consistent.
Processing steps (means, uncertainties, regressions) are carried out appropriately and interpreted correctly.
weaknesses: The claim of âno absolute uncertaintyâ for photogates is unrealistic; manufacturer specs should be used.
Mass measurement uncertainty is ignored and error propagation for theoretical COR is omitted.
Some formatting issues (merged rows, small graph labels) reduce precision and readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of recording and processing is generally clear and precise, uncertainties are considered albeit with notable omissions, and data processing is largely accurate. This aligns with the middle of the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Research question is embedded in a precise physical context with detailed background on momentum and COR.
Apparatus selection (air track, photogates) and variable choices are well justified and linked to measurable quantities.
Method description includes step-by-step instructions, labelled diagrams and a materials list that broadly allow reproduction.
weaknesses: Key methodological details (exact spring compression, photogate sensitivity, justification for five repeats) are omitted, introducing replication ambiguities.
Control variables are identified but lack quantitative monitoring methods and uncertainty budgets.
Minor formatting/wording issues (duplicate 400 g entry, unexplained symbols) detract from clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a specific, appropriate context and provides a broadly reproducible method, but several omissions and limited explanation of control monitoring keep this firmly in the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects solid performance with room to refine methodological detail and clarity.

strengths: The conclusion correctly identifies the trend (COR decreases with mass) and cites graphical and numerical evidence.
Relevant comparison is made to theoretical conservation of momentum values and attributed discrepancies to frictional effects.
weaknesses: An earlier hypothesis predicted an increase in COR, creating a logical inconsistency with the final conclusion.
Comparison to accepted scientific context remains qualitative and lacks quantitative reinforcement (e.g., literature values or measured friction coefficients).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the conclusion is supported by the data, the internal contradiction with the initial hypothesis and only superficial context comparison place it at the lower end of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects valid linkage to analysis but limited consistency and depth.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (launch force variation, airflow fluctuation, mass distribution) and describes their impact on measured COR.
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (lockable launcher, regulated pump power, balanced-mass carts) with explanation of how they mitigate limitations.
weaknesses: Does not quantify the relative impact of each weakness or prioritize which would most improve accuracy.
Improvements are explained qualitatively without feasibility analysis (cost, complexity) or expected quantitative benefit.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes concrete limitations and relevant, realistic improvements, fitting comfortably within the 3â4 band. The absence of quantified impact assessments or feasibility ranking supports a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated, specifies both the independent and dependent variables, and is embedded within a detailed discussion of momentum, conservation laws and the coefficient of restitution for air-track gliders.  The student explains why an air track is used, how COR will be obtained, and why mass is the variable of interest, thereby providing a specific and appropriate physical context rather than a merely generic one.  Minor wording issues (duplicated 400 g entry, tense shifts) do not detract from the overall clarity and focus.

Uncertainties for velocities are estimated using range/2 and propagated to COR; relative and percentage uncertainties are reported and displayed as error bars.  However, the claim that photogates have âno absolute uncertaintyâ is unrealistic, mass uncertainties are ignored, and uncertainty propagation for the theoretical values is omitted.  These significant omissions keep the work in the 3â4 band, lower half.

The student identifies several relevant control variables (track level, air-flow rate, release force, collision point) and briefly states why each matters.  Nevertheless, the discussion stops at description; there is limited explanation of how each control will be monitored quantitatively or how residual variation could influence COR.  Sufficiency of the data set (mass range, 100 g steps, five repeats) is asserted but not justified.  This aligns with a descriptive rather than explanatory treatment.

Three concrete methodological weaknesses are identified (variable launch force, air-flow fluctuation, mass distribution).  For each, the student describes how it could have skewed velocities and affected the trend, demonstrating an understanding of impact but not exploring the magnitude or relative importance in depth.  This descriptive approach meets band 3â4.

Step-by-step instructions, a materials list, distances between photogates, and a labelled sketch give enough information that a competent peer could broadly reproduce the set-up.  However, some details remain ambiguous (exact spring compression length, photogate sensitivity settings, justification for five trials, distance Cart A travels after impact).  These omissions would create minor uncertainty in replication, so the description fits the âreproduced with few ambiguitiesâ level rather than the fully exhaustive 5â6 band.

Averages, uncertainties, COR values and linear regressions are calculated correctly in method if not always flawlessly formatted.  Sample calculations are shown and consistent, and the negative gradient is interpreted.  Minor issuesâe.g., excluding a data point without statistical justification and possible rounding inconsistenciesâconstitute âsome inaccuraciesâ, yet the bulk of the processing is appropriate and accurate.

Corresponding improvements are realistic (spring-loaded launcher with lock, regulated pump power, using balanced-mass carts) and the student explains how each would mitigate the stated limitation.  The explanations, while sensible, remain qualitative and do not prioritise or justify feasibility in detail, placing them securely in the 3â4 band rather than the highest.

Raw data are tabulated with headings, units and significant figures; processed tables separate means and uncertainties, and graphs include labelled axes.  Minor formatting issues (merged rows, truncated headings, small graph images) reduce precision, yet overall the presentation is clear and largely precise, meeting the 3â4 band.

Experimental results are compared with theoretical values derived from conservation of momentum, gradients are contrasted and discrepancies attributed to friction and air-flow variation.  While the discussion is relevant and more than superficial, it stops short of a fully reasoned, quantitative reconciliation; therefore, it aligns with the 3â4 descriptor.

The student states that COR decreases with increasing mass and references both the graph and numerical gradient, demonstrating clear linkage to the processed data.  However, earlier in the report the prediction was that COR would increase, revealing a logical inconsistency.  Despite this, the final conclusion is largely supported by the analysis, fitting the middle band rather than the fully consistent 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: The research question contains a duplicated mass value (400 g) and inconsistent formatting within the inline list. Clarify and correct the mass series to avoid ambiguity in the investigated parameters.
The background on momentum effectively situates the research within conservation laws. The explanation of momentumâs vector nature and its relevance to collisions is clear and precise.
Quantifying momentum transfer by varying mass and measuring before/after velocities is well justified. This links the research question to measurable physical quantities.
The COR equation and its derivation are clearly presented. The ratio of separation to approach velocity is stated with correct absolute-value notation.
The algebraic derivation in equations (1) and (2) is logically correct but the use of the tilde symbol (~) is unexplained. Specify its meaning or remove it.
Switching from momentum-based to energy-based COR formula is not justified here. Explain why the kinetic-energy definition is preferred over the velocity ratio in this context.
The conclusion in the background predicts that COR increases with mass, which contradicts later findings. Align the hypothesis with final results or explain the reversal.
When describing mass measurement, include the uncertainty of the digital scale to provide a complete error budget for the independent variable.
The control variables are identified but lack quantitative monitoring methods. Describe how track level and airflow rate will be measured and recorded.
Specify the vacuum pumpâs flow rate or pressure setting rather than âhighest powerâ to ensure exact reproducibility of airflow-induced friction reduction.
The photogate placement at 10 cm and 30 cm is clear, but justify why these distances optimize velocity measurement relative to collision dynamics.
Declaring photogates have âno absolute uncertaintyâ is unrealistic. Include manufacturer specifications for timing resolution to estimate a more accurate uncertainty.
Many table rows omit the mass label, causing reader confusion. Repeat the mass value for each trial entry or use merged cells consistently.
Sample average velocity calculation is correctly executed, with proper formula and values. This demonstrates clear data processing.
Using range/2 for uncertainty neglects instrument error. Incorporate photogate timing resolution into uncertainty calculation for greater precision.
The relative-uncertainty propagation formula appears to double-count factors of two. Re-express the derivation step-by-step to verify each term.
Theoretical COR values lack uncertainty propagation. Include error analysis on theoretical velocities to compare experimental and theoretical uncertainty ranges.
Theoretical COR graphâs axes and units are too small to read. Improve label size and include error bars for a clearer comparison.
Excluding the 100 g data point is defensible, but provide a statistical criterion (e.g., residual analysis) to justify it as an outlier.
The conclusion states that COR decreases with mass, contradicting an earlier hypothesis of increase. Clarify and resolve this inconsistency.
Comparison to theoretical context mentions friction qualitatively. Strengthen by referencing measured friction coefficients or literature COR values for air-track collisions.
Identifying the spring compression variation as a limitation shows good critical insight into launch consistency.
Suggesting a weight-and-pulley system is realistic; discuss feasibility (cost, setup complexity) and how it would quantitatively reduce variation.
The risk assessment omits hazards from spring recoil. Include potential risks from compressed springs and protective measures.
The materials list includes two springs but method uses springs on cart A only. Clarify spring function and whether both springs are used.
Citing BYJUâS for inertia is acceptable but consider a peer-reviewed textbook or journal source for stronger theoretical grounding.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
